origin.question: 15.20. Show that the Schwarzschild metric is also a solution of the Brans-Dicke theory of gravity. (For the Brans-Dicke field equations, see e.g. MTW p. 1070.)
origin.answer: The Schwarzschild metric is a solution of the vacuum Brans-Dicke equations.
origin.solution:
Solution 15.20. The Brans-Dicke field equations can be put into the form$$\mathrm {G}^{\mu 
u} + \mathrm {F}^{\mu 
u} \left(\Phi_{; \alpha}, \Phi_{; \alpha}  etaight) = \mathrm {T}^{\mu 
u}, 	ag {1a}$$$$\square \Phi = T_{\mu}^{\mu} 	ag {1b}$$where every term of $\mathbf{F}^{\mu 
u}$ is proportional to derivatives of the scalar field $\Phi$ . In vacuum, a solution of Equation (1b) is$$\Phi = 	ext {c o n s t a n t}. 	ag {2}$$Substituting Equation (2) into (1a), one obtains$$\mathbf {G}^{\mu 
u} = 0,$$which are the vacuum Einstein equation. Consequently, the Schwarzschild metric is a solution of the vacuum Brans-Dicke equations. (The most general static, spherically symmetric vacuum metric in Brans-Dicke theory has two arbitrary parameters corresponding to mass and scalar charge. A spherical black hole has zero scalar charge and is the Schwarzschild solution.)
